name: William Laws Calley Jr.
role_title: First Lieutenant, U.S. Army, Platoon Leader, C Company, 1st Battalion, 20th Infantry, Americal Division
charges: Premeditated murder of 22 unarmed South Vietnamese civilians (6 specifications, Article 118 UCMJ); assault with intent to murder (My Lai Massacre)
outcome: Sentence reduced to 10 years; served ~3 years house arrest
source1_description: Army Criminal Investigation Division My Lai records
source2_description: National Archives My Lai Massacre documentation

name: Aldrich Hazen Ames
role_title: CIA Operations Officer, Chief of Counterintelligence, Soviet/East European Division
charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage, espionage, tax evasion — passed classified information to the Soviet Union/Russia 1985–1993, compromising dozens of CIA assets (died in federal custody Jan. 5, 2026)
outcome: Life without parole (died in custody January 5, 2026)
source1_description: CIA official history on Aldrich Ames espionage case

name: Jonathan Jay Pollard
role_title: Civilian Intelligence Analyst, Naval Investigative Service / Naval Intelligence
charges: Conspiracy to deliver national defense information to a foreign government — passed classified U.S. military documents to Israel over 17 months
outcome: Life in prison; paroled November 20, 2015 after 30 years
source1_description: DOJ documentation on Pollard espionage case
source2_description: Naval Criminal Investigative Service case file

name: Charles A. Graner Jr.
role_title: Specialist, U.S. Army Reserve, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard, Iraq
charges: Conspiracy to maltreat detainees, assault, battery, dereliction of duty, maltreatment of detainees, committing indecent acts, obstruction of justice
outcome: 10 years; paroled Aug. 6, 2011; dishonorable discharge
source1_description: Army Criminal Investigation Division Abu Ghraib investigation
source2_description: Department of Defense Abu Ghraib court-martial records

name: Lynndie Rana England
role_title: Private First Class, U.S. Army Reserve, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard, Iraq
charges: One count conspiracy, four counts maltreating detainees, one count committing an indecent act (iconic Abu Ghraib photos)
outcome: 3 years; released on parole Mar. 1, 2007; dishonorable discharge
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib investigation documentation

name: Ivan Lee Frederick II
role_title: Staff Sergeant, U.S. Army Reserve, 372nd Military Police Company; senior enlisted at Abu Ghraib's hard-site, Iraq
charges: Conspiracy, dereliction of duty, maltreatment of detainees, assault, committing an indecent act (guilty plea to 5 charges)
outcome: 8 years; released on parole Oct. 2007; dishonorable discharge
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib case file

name: Sabrina D. Harman
role_title: Specialist, U.S. Army Reserve, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard, Iraq
charges: Dereliction of duty, maltreatment of detainees, assault, committing an indecent act (convicted on 6 of 7 counts)
outcome: 6 months; bad conduct discharge; reduction in rank
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib documentation

name: Jeremy C. Sivits
role_title: Specialist, U.S. Army, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard — first Abu Ghraib soldier tried
charges: Dereliction of duty, maltreatment of detainees, assault (pleaded guilty at special court-martial; died of COVID-19 in 2022)
outcome: 1 year; bad conduct discharge; reduction to Private (died 2022)
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib case file

name: Javal Davis
role_title: Sergeant, U.S. Army, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard, Iraq
charges: Dereliction of duty, making false official statements, battery (stepped on and fell on handcuffed detainees) — guilty plea
outcome: 6 months; reduction to Private; bad conduct discharge
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib investigation

name: Santos Cardona
role_title: Sergeant, U.S. Army, 16th Military Police Brigade; dog handler at Abu Ghraib prison, Iraq
charges: Dereliction of duty, aggravated assault (misusing military dog against detainees; killed in action in Afghanistan 2009 as government contractor)
jurisdiction: General court-martial, Fort Bragg, North Carolina
outcome: 90 days hard labor; fined; demoted; unable to re-enlist (killed in action 2009)
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib documentation

name: Roman Krol
role_title: Specialist, U.S. Army, 325th Military Intelligence Battalion; Abu Ghraib prison, Iraq
charges: Conspiracy to maltreat detainees, maltreatment of detainees (pleaded guilty)
outcome: 10 months; reduction to Private; bad conduct discharge
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib case file

name: Michael Smith
role_title: Sergeant, U.S. Army, 523rd Military Police Battalion; dog handler at Abu Ghraib prison, Iraq
charges: Two counts prisoner maltreatment, simple assault, conspiracy to maltreat, dereliction of duty, committing an indecent act
outcome: 179 days; $2,250 fine; demotion to Private; bad conduct discharge
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib investigation

name: Megan Ambuhl
role_title: Specialist (later Private), U.S. Army Reserve, 372nd Military Police Company; Abu Ghraib prison guard, Iraq (subsequently married Charles Graner)
charges: Dereliction of duty (convicted on one count after court-martial)
outcome: Dishonorable discharge; reduction to Private; forfeiture of half-month's pay; no prison time
source1_description: Army CID Abu Ghraib case file

name: Calvin Gibbs
role_title: Staff Sergeant, U.S. Army, 3rd Platoon, 2nd Battalion, 1st Infantry Regiment, 5th Stryker Brigade; Afghanistan Maywand District Kill Team — ringleader
charges: Three counts premeditated murder, conspiracy to commit murder, illegally severing fingers/teeth/leg bones from Afghan corpses as trophies, planting weapons on victims, dereliction of duty, drug use (15 total counts)
jurisdiction: General court-martial, Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington
outcome: Life with possibility of parole after 9 years; dishonorable discharge
source1_description: Army CID Maywand District Kill Team investigation

name: Jeremy N. Morlock
role_title: Specialist (Corporal), U.S. Army, 2nd Battalion, 1st Infantry Regiment, 5th Stryker Brigade; Maywand District Kill Team member, Afghanistan
charges: Three counts premeditated murder, conspiracy, obstruction of justice, drug use (pleaded guilty)
jurisdiction: General court-martial, Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington
outcome: 24 years; reduction to Private; dishonorable discharge
source1_description: Army CID Maywand District Kill Team case file

name: Andrew H. Holmes
role_title: Private First Class, U.S. Army, 2nd Battalion, 1st Infantry Regiment, 5th Stryker Brigade; Maywand District Kill Team member, Afghanistan
charges: Murder without premeditation (guilty plea — killing of unarmed 15-year-old Afghan boy)
jurisdiction: General court-martial, Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington
outcome: 7 years; released Oct. 2015 for good behavior
source1_description: Army CID Maywand District investigation

name: Chelsea Elizabeth Manning
role_title: Private First Class (later Specialist), U.S. Army, Intelligence Analyst, 2nd Brigade Combat Team, 10th Mountain Division; Iraq
charges: 6 counts espionage (Espionage Act), 5 counts theft, 2 counts computer fraud, multiple UCMJ infractions — leaked ~750,000 classified documents/diplomatic cables to WikiLeaks
outcome: 35 years; sentence commuted by President Obama Jan. 17, 2017; released May 17, 2017
source1_description: Army court-martial records for Manning case

name: Joshua Adam Schulte
role_title: CIA Operations Officer / Software Engineer, Center for Cyber Intelligence (CCI)
charges: 8 counts: illegal gathering and transmission of national defense information, unauthorized computer access (Vault 7 — leaked entire CIA hacking tool cache to WikiLeaks, largest CIA data breach in history); child pornography charges
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, S.D. New York
outcome: 40 years: 400 months espionage + 80 months child pornography; lifetime supervised release
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Schulte sentencing

name: Ana Belén Montes
role_title: Senior Analyst, U.S. Defense Intelligence Agency (DIA); spied for Cuba 1985–2001 — 'Queen of Cuba'
charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage on behalf of the Cuban government
outcome: 25 years; released Jan. 6, 2023 after 20 years for good behavior
source1_description: FBI famous cases: Ana Montes
source2_description: DOJ Press Release on Montes sentencing

name: Larry Wu-Tai Chin
role_title: CIA Foreign Broadcast Information Service Analyst and Translator; spied for China 1952–1985
charges: 17 counts including conspiracy to commit espionage, passing classified documents to China, failure to report foreign bank income — betrayed Korean War POW locations to China (committed suicide in custody Feb. 22, 1986 before sentencing)
outcome: Died by suicide in custody before sentencing (convicted Feb. 7, 1986)
conviction_date: 1986 (convicted Feb. 7; died in custody before sentencing)
source1_description: FBI famous cases: Larry Wu-Tai Chin
source2_description: CIA history on Chin espionage case

name: Earl Edwin Pitts
role_title: FBI Special Agent, counterintelligence (New York field office and FBI HQ); sold secrets to KGB/SVR 1987–1992
charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage, attempted espionage — sold classified counterintelligence materials and U.S. agent identities to the Soviet Union and Russia for $224,000+
outcome: 27 years in federal prison
source1_description: FBI official history on Earl Pitts espionage case
source2_description: DOJ Press Release on Pitts sentencing

name: Jeffrey Alexander Sterling
role_title: CIA Operations Officer, Clandestine Service; fired 2002
charges: 9 counts: unauthorized disclosure of national defense information (Espionage Act); obstruction of justice; mail fraud; unauthorized conveyance of government property — disclosed Operation Merlin (Iran nuclear deception program) to journalist James Risen
outcome: 42 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Sterling sentencing

name: David Howell Petraeus
role_title: Retired General (4-star), U.S. Army; Director of the CIA, 2011–2012; former Commander, U.S. Central Command
charges: Unauthorized removal and retention of classified material (misdemeanor) — shared 8 'Black Books' of top-secret notebooks with his biographer/mistress Paula Broadwell; made false statements to FBI
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, W.D. North Carolina
outcome: 2 years probation; $100,000 fine
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Petraeus guilty plea

name: Brian Patrick Regan
role_title: Retired Master Sergeant, U.S. Air Force; signals intelligence analyst, National Reconnaissance Office (NRO)
charges: Two counts attempted espionage, one count gathering national defense information — buried 20,000+ pages of classified documents in parklands; attempted to sell secrets to Iraq, China, and Libya
outcome: Life without parole
source1_description: FBI famous cases: Brian Regan
source2_description: DOJ Press Release on Regan sentencing

name: Terry J. Albury
role_title: FBI Special Agent, Minneapolis Field Office
charges: Unauthorized disclosure and retention of national defense information (Espionage Act) — leaked classified documents on FBI informant use and surveillance practices to The Intercept
outcome: 4 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Albury guilty plea

name: Shamai Kedem Leibowitz
role_title: FBI Contract Linguist / Hebrew translator, Counterintelligence Division
charges: Unlawfully providing classified documents to an unauthorized person (Espionage Act) — leaked five classified documents to a blogger
outcome: 20 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Leibowitz sentencing

name: Clyde Lee Conrad
role_title: Sergeant First Class, U.S. Army, G3 War Plans Section, 8th Infantry Division, West Germany; sold NATO secrets 1974–1985
charges: High treason and espionage — sold over 30,000 classified documents including NATO wartime defense plans and nuclear weapons site locations to Warsaw Pact for ~$1.2 million (died in German prison Jan. 8, 1998)
jurisdiction: Koblenz State Appellate Court, West Germany
outcome: Life imprisonment; died in prison 1998
source1_description: Army CID Conrad espionage investigation
source2_description: German court documentation on Conrad treason case

name: Jonathan Toebbe
role_title: Nuclear Engineer, Naval Reactors Program, U.S. Navy (civilian employee)
charges: Conspiracy to communicate restricted data (Atomic Energy Act) — attempted to sell Virginia-class submarine nuclear propulsion secrets to a foreign government (FBI undercover agent)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, N.D. West Virginia
outcome: 19 years 4 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Toebbe sentencing

name: Jack Douglas Teixeira
role_title: Airman First Class / Senior Airman, 102nd Intelligence Wing, Massachusetts Air National Guard; IT support specialist with Top Secret access
charges: Six counts willful retention and transmission of classified national defense information (Espionage Act) — leaked hundreds of Top Secret Pentagon documents to Discord chat group 2022–2023 (one of largest U.S. classified leaks ever)
outcome: 15 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Teixeira sentencing

name: Rear Admiral Robert Gilbeau
role_title: Rear Admiral, U.S. Navy (active duty); Special Assistant to Chief of the Navy Supply Corps; first Navy admiral convicted of a federal crime while on active duty — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Making false official statements — lied to federal investigators about 20-year relationship with 'Fat Leonard' Francis and receipt of gifts/prostitutes
outcome: 18 months; $100,000 fine; reduced to Captain upon retirement
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Gilbeau sentencing
source2_description: Navy Times coverage of Fat Leonard case

name: Captain Daniel Dusek
role_title: Captain (O-6), U.S. Navy; former Deputy Director of Operations, Seventh Fleet; former Commander, USS Bonhomme Richard — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — steered aircraft carriers to Fat Leonard's ports in exchange for prostitutes, luxury travel, cash; hand-delivered classified ship schedules to GDMA
outcome: 46 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Dusek sentencing

name: Commander Michael Misiewicz
role_title: Commander (O-5), U.S. Navy; former Deputy Director of Operations, Seventh Fleet — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy and bribery — provided classified ship movements (including submarines) and ballistic-missile defense plans to GDMA in exchange for prostitutes, cash, luxury hotel stays, airfare
outcome: 78 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Misiewicz sentencing

name: Captain Michael George Brooks
role_title: Captain (O-6, retired), U.S. Navy; Naval Attaché, U.S. Embassy Manila — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — passed confidential Navy documents to GDMA; permitted GDMA to write its own performance evaluations; accepted prostitutes, wine, hotel rooms, electronics, luxury watches
outcome: 3.5 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Brooks sentencing

name: Commander Gentry Debord
role_title: Lieutenant Commander (O-4), U.S. Navy; logistics officer, USS Essex — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — assisted GDMA submitting fake invoices, tipped off about Navy investigation, accepted travel, entertainment, prostitutes
outcome: 30 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Debord sentencing

name: Captain David Haas
role_title: Captain (O-6, retired), U.S. Navy; former Director of Seventh Fleet Maritime Operations aboard USS Blue Ridge — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — took ~$145,000 in bribes; participated in prostitution parties; helped recruit additional Navy co-conspirators
outcome: 2.5 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Haas sentencing

name: Captain Jesus V. Cantu
role_title: Captain (O-6, retired), U.S. Navy; Assistant Chief of Staff for Logistics, 7th Fleet — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — accepted bribes and prostitutes through 2013
outcome: 30 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Cantu sentencing

name: Paul Simpkins
role_title: Civilian Navy contracting supervisor, Singapore — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Bribery, conspiracy to commit bribery — accepted $350,000 in cash bribes; protected GDMA contracts and blocked fraud investigations
outcome: 6 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Simpkins sentencing

name: Commander Troy Amundson
role_title: Commander (O-5, retired), U.S. Navy; former officer, USS Halsey — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — provided proprietary Navy information to GDMA in exchange for dinners, drinks, transportation, and prostitutes
outcome: 2.5 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Amundson sentencing

name: Master Chief Ricarte Icmat David
role_title: Master Chief Petty Officer (E-9, retired), U.S. Navy; various 7th Fleet logistics posts — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit honest services fraud — approved fraudulently inflated invoices; passed classified information to GDMA; accepted cash, hotel rooms, and prostitutes
outcome: 17 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on David sentencing

name: Captain Jeffrey Breslau
role_title: Captain (O-6, retired), U.S. Navy; Pacific Fleet Public Affairs Director, 2009–2012 — Fat Leonard scandal
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery — accepted $65,000 from Francis for ghostwriting materials and providing confidential Navy information; sent 135 emails on Francis's behalf
outcome: 6 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Breslau sentencing

name: Stephen Jin-Woo Kim
role_title: Senior Adviser for Intelligence, State Department contractor
charges: Unauthorized disclosure of national defense information (Espionage Act) — leaked Top Secret/SCI intelligence assessment about North Korea's likely response to UN sanctions to Fox News reporter James Rosen
outcome: 13 months in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Kim sentencing

name: Major General William T. Cooley
role_title: Major General (O-8), U.S. Air Force; Commander, Air Force Research Laboratory, Wright-Patterson AFB — first Air Force general in service history convicted at court-martial
charges: Abusive sexual contact under Article 120, UCMJ (kissed sister-in-law without consent)
jurisdiction: General court-martial, Wright-Patterson Air Force Base, Ohio
outcome: Written reprimand; forfeiture of ~$50,000 in pay; no prison time; reduced to Colonel upon retirement
source1_description: Air Force court-martial documentation
source2_description: DOD news coverage of Cooley case

name: Major John L. Cockerham
role_title: Major (O-4), U.S. Army; contracting officer, Camp Arifjan, Kuwait — ringleader of Iraq/Kuwait contract bribery scheme
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery, bribery, money laundering — accepted ~$9.6 million in bribes for bottled water and other contracts supporting U.S. Army operations in Iraq and Kuwait
outcome: 17.5 years; $9.6 million fines/restitution — longest sentence given to any U.S. service member for Iraq/Afghanistan war fraud at the time
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Cockerham sentencing

name: Vice Admiral Robert Burke
role_title: Vice Chief of Naval Operations; former Commander, U.S. Naval Forces Europe-Africa
charges: Bribery — accepted a $500,000-per-year job from defense contractor Leidos in exchange for directing Navy contracts and personnel actions toward that contractor while still on active duty
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia
outcome: Convicted on all counts by jury; sentenced to 30 months in federal prison, January 2025. Highest-ranking Navy officer convicted of bribery.
source1_description: DOJ — Vice Admiral Robert Burke convicted of bribery
source2_description: AP News — 4-star admiral found guilty of bribery

name: Chelsea Manning
role_title: U.S. Army Intelligence Analyst, 10th Mountain Division
charges: Espionage Act violations (6 counts), theft of government property, computer fraud — leaked 750,000 classified diplomatic cables, military logs, and videos to WikiLeaks including the 'Collateral Murder' video
outcome: Sentenced to 35 years confinement (August 21, 2013); sentence commuted to 7 years by President Obama on January 17, 2017. Released May 17, 2017.
source1_description: Army Times — Chelsea Manning sentence: 35 years
source2_description: The Guardian — Obama commutes Chelsea Manning sentence
notes: Acquitted of the most serious charge: aiding the enemy. Sentence commuted by President Obama on January 17, 2017 (same day as Inauguration). Released May 17, 2017.

name: Leonard "Fat Leonard" Francis
role_title: CEO, Glenn Defense Marine Asia (GDMA) — U.S. Navy ship servicing contractor
charges: Bribery of U.S. naval officers; conspiracy to commit bribery; wire fraud — bribed more than 30 officers with prostitutes, luxury travel, and cash in exchange for classified ship schedules and routing of Navy vessels to his ports
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California
outcome: Pleaded guilty 2015; awaiting sentencing, escaped house arrest in 2022 and fled to Venezuela, recaptured in Venezuela 2023, returned to U.S. Sentenced to 15 years in federal prison, September 2023.
source1_description: DOJ SDCA — 'Fat Leonard' sentenced to 15 years in prison
source2_description: Reuters — 'Fat Leonard' sentenced after 2022 escape and recapture
notes: The 'Fat Leonard' scandal is considered the worst corruption scandal in U.S. Navy history. Over 30 Navy officers were charged or investigated, with multiple convictions.
